ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper combines two established ideas—shared/private multi-task architectures and adversarial training for invariant representation—into a single framework (ASPMTL) and extends adversarial discrimination to a multi-class task-discriminator. That extension and the specific LSTM-based instantiation are a non-trivial engineering contribution, but conceptually they largely recombine known components rather than introduce a fundamentally new perspective. From my theory-first view the work would be more novel if it included formal or mechanistic analysis explaining when and why the adversarial shared/private split improves transfer; as presented the contribution reads as an incremental but useful advance rather than a breakthrough. | B(3): This paper combines established components (shared/private LSTM encoders, gradient reversal adversarial training) but applies them in a specific way—using a multi-class task discriminator to enforce task-invariance in the shared space—which is a sensible and practical engineering extension rather than a radical new idea. The problem (multi-task text classification with shared/private representations) is well-studied, and the method is a non-trivial but incremental extension of prior adversarial and shared-private MTL work. Given the clear empirical validation across 16 datasets, the contribution is useful and informative to practitioners but not highly novel from a methodological perspective.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines two established ideas—shared/private multi-task architectures and adversarial training for invariant representation—into a single framework (ASPMTL) and extends adversarial discrimination to a multi-class task-discriminator. That extension and the specific LSTM-based instantiation are a non-trivial engineering contribution, but conceptually they largely recombine known components rather than introduce a fundamentally new perspective. From my theory-first view the work would be more novel if it included formal or mechanistic analysis explaining when and why the adversarial shared/private split improves transfer; as presented the contribution reads as an incremental but useful advance rather than a breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper combines established components (shared/private LSTM encoders, gradient reversal adversarial training) but applies them in a specific way—using a multi-class task discriminator to enforce task-invariance in the shared space—which is a sensible and practical engineering extension rather than a radical new idea. The problem (multi-task text classification with shared/private representations) is well-studied, and the method is a non-trivial but incremental extension of prior adversarial and shared-private MTL work. Given the clear empirical validation across 16 datasets, the contribution is useful and informative to practitioners but not highly novel from a methodological perspective.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: an adversarial shared-private MTL with a multi-class discriminator. Notation and definitions appear consistent, figures/tables (per-task error rates across 16 datasets) effectively communicate results, and writing quality is reported as good. From a theory-first perspective I note minor presentation omissions — e.g., no mention of released code or algorithmic pseudocode in the summary — which slightly reduce reproducibility clarity, so I deduct one point. | B(4): Paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early, making it easy to grasp the proposed adversarial shared-private MTL idea. Notation appears consistent and well defined, and figures/tables (per-task error rates across 16 datasets) effectively support the empirical claims; hyperparameters and related work are also clearly reported. Writing quality is good with little ambiguity, though a couple of methodological details (e.g., optimization/implementation nuances) could be expanded for complete reproducibility, keeping it from a perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early: an adversarial shared-private MTL with a multi-class discriminator. Notation and definitions appear consistent, figures/tables (per-task error rates across 16 datasets) effectively communicate results, and writing quality is reported as good. From a theory-first perspective I note minor presentation omissions — e.g., no mention of released code or algorithmic pseudocode in the summary — which slightly reduce reproducibility clarity, so I deduct one point.

reviewer_b.justification: Paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the main contributions early, making it easy to grasp the proposed adversarial shared-private MTL idea. Notation appears consistent and well defined, and figures/tables (per-task error rates across 16 datasets) effectively support the empirical claims; hyperparameters and related work are also clearly reported. Writing quality is good with little ambiguity, though a couple of methodological details (e.g., optimization/implementation nuances) could be expanded for complete reproducibility, keeping it from a perfect score.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates consistent empirical gains across many datasets, making it likely to be adopted by practitioners. The approach is pragmatic and implementable (shared/private LSTMs with a multi-class adversarial discriminator), but its contribution is primarily empirical and architectural rather than theoretical, and lack of mentioned code limits immediate reproducibility—factors that temper but do not negate its notable practical impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—separating shared vs. task-specific features in multi-task text classification—and shows consistent empirical gains across 16 datasets, which suggests usefulness to practitioners. However, from a theory-first perspective the contribution is primarily empirical and architectural (multi-class adversarial discriminator + shared/private LSTMs) without formal analysis of when or why the approach prevents contamination, and code release is not mentioned which limits reproducibility and adoption. Thus the work is a useful, moderate-impact advance that will inform follow-up empirical work but is unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers think about representation learning without deeper mechanistic or theoretical support.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important practical problem of separating task-invariant and task-specific features in multi-task text classification using a simple, implementable shared-private LSTM architecture with a multi-class adversarial discriminator and gradient-reversal training. The empirical evaluation across 16 diverse datasets (14 Amazon domains + IMDB + MR) showing consistent error-rate improvements and evidence of transferable shared knowledge makes it likely practitioners will adopt or build on the approach. While not a conceptual revolution, the method is a pragmatic, scalable improvement that should influence how multi-task models are engineered in NLP.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a sensible optimization and compelling empirical gains across 16 tasks, showing the method is practically effective. However, they also agree important methodological elements are missing—no theoretical justification, no seed variability/confidence intervals, ablations or probes linking mechanism to claimed disentanglement, and no mention of released code. Given the strong empirical results but these gaps in rigor, a score of 4 reflects confidence in the main claims while noting the limitations that prevent full verification.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s primary empirical claim—that ASPMTL (shared/private LSTMs + multi-class adversarial discriminator + difference loss) improves average error across 16 text-classification tasks—is supported by experiments against reasonable baselines, but I see no theoretical analysis explaining why the multi-class adversary or the difference penalty should reliably produce disentangled shared/private representations. There are no proofs or formal guarantees, and important experimental rigor is missing: no report of seed variability, confidence intervals, or mechanistic diagnostics (e.g., probes of shared vs private spaces) that would link the method to the claimed mechanism. Given these gaps the conclusions are plausible but not fully justified by the presented evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by a strong empirical evaluation across 16 diverse text-classification tasks showing consistent gains versus reasonable baselines (single-task LSTM, SP/FS-MTL, MT-CNN/MT-DNN). The optimization approach (shared/private LSTMs + multi-class adversarial discriminator implemented with a gradient-reversal layer) is sensible and correctly applied; there are no obvious mathematical errors in the described setup. My main reservations are practical: the paper does not mention released code and I did not see evidence of statistical significance testing or ablations that isolate each loss term’s contribution, which would strengthen confidence. Overall the empirical evidence is compelling, with only minor methodological gaps that don’t overturn the conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is strong (16 datasets, broad baselines) and the adversarial shared/private design is convincing, but both note missing ablations, sensitivity analyses, statistical robustness checks, and absence of released code. These are important reproducibility and diagnostic gaps but do not negate the core empirical contribution, so I rate it slightly above average while flagging those limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental setup is reasonably comprehensive in datasets and baselines (16 text-classification tasks and several prior MTL models), which supports the empirical claim of improved error rates. However, the paper lacks critical ablation/sensitivity analyses and statistical robustness checks (no reported seed variability or confidence intervals) and provides no mechanistic diagnostics probing the shared vs private spaces. Related work appears well covered and one limitation (FS-MTL degradation) is noted, but reproducibility is weak—code is not mentioned and only hyperparameters are reported without algorithmic pseudocode—preventing full verification.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall a strong empirical paper: evaluation on 16 diverse text-classification datasets and comparisons to a broad set of baselines (single-task LSTM, BiLSTM, sLSTM, FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN) gives convincing evidence for the proposed adversarial shared/private design. The paper reports hyperparameters and uses a clear loss decomposition (LTask + λLAdv + γLDiff) and implements optimization with gradient reversal, but the summary does not indicate systematic ablations or sensitivity analyses over λ/γ nor explicit breakdowns of when/why the model fails. Related work coverage is thorough, yet reproducibility is only partially addressed (no code mentioned) and discussion of negative results or limitations is limited, so I rate it solid with minor gaps.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a core, mainstream NLP/ML topic and uses appropriate empirical evaluation on a large, diverse set of datasets with standard baselines and protocols, making it a good fit for a top venue. Reviewer A’s concerns about missing theoretical analysis and explicit code release are valid but do not undermine the paper’s clear relevance and strong empirical contribution, so I rate APPROPRIATENESS at the highest level.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a mainstream NLP/ML topic — multi-task representation learning for sentence classification — and evaluates on a large suite of 16 well-known text-classification datasets against appropriate baselines, so its audience and empirical evaluation are broadly within scope for a top venue. As a theory-first reviewer I note the contribution is primarily empirical (an adversarial shared/private architecture with a multi-class discriminator) and the summary does not report formal analysis of when or why the method should separate task-invariant vs task-specific features, nor is code release mentioned; these omissions reduce its appeal for venues that prize mechanistic insight and reproducibility. Overall it is a good fit for a top conference but would be stronger with theoretical justification, diagnostic analyses of the shared/private spaces, and publicly released code/configs.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—multi-task representation learning with adversarial disentangling—and uses standard, well-understood components (shared/private LSTMs, gradient reversal) making it directly relevant to top venues. The evaluation is empirical and pragmatic (16 diverse text classification datasets, multiple strong baselines, per-task error rates, and reported hyperparameters), which is precisely the kind of evidence the community expects. The problem formulation and experimental protocol match venue standards and the work is clearly positioned within the multi-task/transfer learning literature, so I view it as a very good fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against a broad, relevant set of baselines on the same 16 datasets with consistent metrics and experimental setup, supporting fair and comprehensive empirical evaluation. Reviewer A's concerns about limited mechanistic diagnostics and no mention of code release are valid caveats, but they do not undermine the overall fairness and completeness of the comparisons. Given the thorough baseline coverage and clear analysis of the proposed adversarial/shared-private distinction, a top score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates ASPMTL against a broad set of sensible baselines (single-task LSTM variants, shared/private multi-task models, MT-CNN, MT-DNN) on the same 16 datasets and reports error rates, so comparisons are largely appropriate and fair. However, the summary does not indicate whether the authors acknowledge instances where prior methods match or beat their approach, nor does it report mechanistic diagnostics linking the adversarial/shared-private design to gains; code release is also not mentioned. Related work coverage is described as thorough, so overall comparisons are good but missing some transparency and deeper analysis of when/why improvements occur.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad and relevant set of baselines (single-task LSTM, BiLSTM, sLSTM, FS-MTL, SP-MTL, MT-CNN, MT-DNN) on the same 16 text-classification datasets with consistent error-rate metrics and reported hyperparameters, which supports fair experimental conditions. It also acknowledges when fully-shared MTL can hurt performance and discusses related work thoroughly, and the differences between the shared/private baselines and the proposed adversarial extension are clearly analyzed—overall a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison by practical standards.

main_claims: Propose an adversarial multi-task learning framework (ASPMTL) that separates shared and private latent feature spaces to reduce contamination between task-invariant and task-specific features.
Extend adversarial training to a multi-class task-discriminator to encourage the shared feature space to be task-invariant.

Demonstrate empirically on 16 text classification tasks that the proposed ASPMTL outperforms single-task and prior multi-task baselines, yielding substantial average error-rate improvements and transferable shared knowledge.
_summary.methodology: Use LSTM-based sentence encoders with a shared LSTM and per-task private LSTMs (shared-private architecture), and train a multi-class task discriminator adversarially against the shared encoder; final loss L = LTask + λ LAdv + γ LDiff and optimization implemented via a gradient reversal layer.
datasets: 16 text classification datasets: 14 Amazon product-review datasets (various domains including Books, DVDs, Electronics, etc. collected from Blitzer et al. (2007)), IMDB movie reviews (Maas et al., 2011), MR (rotten tomatoes) (Pang and Lee, 2005)
limitations: The fully-shared multi-task model (FS-MTL) can degrade performance on some tasks by mixing private and shared information.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Critic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a focus on representation learning theory and formal guarantees for deep models.
traits: Requires formal or at least rigorous theoretical justification for new architectural claims; will penalize papers that introduce new objectives/architectures without an analysis of when/why they should work.
Subtracts points if no statistical significance or robust sensitivity analysis is reported (e.g., ablations across seeds, hyperparameter sweeps, confidence intervals).
Penalizes empirical papers that do not provide diagnostics linking mechanism to claimed effect (e.g., no probing of learned shared vs private spaces or failure modes).
Demands clear reproducibility artifacts: pseudo-code or algorithmic description beyond standard components, and public release of code/configs; deducts for missing details preventing replication.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Skeptical and conservative: favors papers with theoretical insight or mechanistic analysis and downgrades empirically-focused work lacking rigorous explanations or reproducibility.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in NLP/representation learning with extensive experience building multi-task and transfer learning systems for real-world text classification.
traits: Rewards strong empirical demonstrations on many real tasks (positively biases papers reporting consistent gains across >=10 diverse datasets).

Favors practical, easily-implementable method improvements (e.g., use of standard components like LSTMs + gradient reversal) and will give credit for empirical transferability evidence (e.g., improvements when adding new tasks).
Values multi-task learning innovations that are simple to adopt and scale; will mark up for clear runtime/parameter trade-offs or modest added complexity relative to baseline.
Credits papers that introduce sensible architectural/optimization tweaks with measurable average-case improvements even without formal proofs; less concerned about deep theoretical justification.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and outcome-driven: rewards clear empirical gains and practical applicability, tolerating limited theory if results are compelling and method is usable.
